title.scholar: John Brown's Raid on Harpers Ferry
title.explorer: A Radical Plan to End Slavery!

summary.scholar: On October 16, 1859, abolitionist John Brown led 21 men in a raid on the federal armory at Harpers Ferry, Virginia (now West Virginia), hoping to spark a slave insurrection. The raid was quickly suppressed by U.S. Marines led by Colonel Robert E. Lee. Brown was tried and hanged, but he became a martyr for the abolitionist cause. His raid terrified the South and deepened the sectional crisis that led to the Civil War.
summary.explorer: In 1859, a man named John Brown tried something really bold — he attacked a government weapons building in Harpers Ferry, hoping to start a rebellion to free enslaved people. He was captured by soldiers led by Robert E. Lee and was executed. But many people in the North considered him a hero, while the South was terrified. The Civil War was getting closer.

significance.scholar: Brown's raid polarized the nation — making him a martyr in the North and confirming Southern fears of Northern aggression, pushing the country closer to civil war.
significance.explorer: John Brown was willing to die to end slavery. His raid made it clear that the argument over slavery couldn't be solved peacefully.

sources.citation: Miller Center: U.S. Presidents and Key Events
